this uh podcast is called the Credit Card BS podcast because it reflects the schools that Shawn and I attend,

credit cards. So today's topic is going to be a very fun one. Uh, the title is Hot Take: The Chase Trifecta is
overrated. Uh, so let's begin with Sean. Why don't you tell uh everyone just
() 
really quickly what a trifecta system is? Yeah, so the Chase trifecta or the typical idea of it is you'd have three
Chase cards. So you'd have one of your rotating category cards like the Chase Freedom Flex. You'd have your basic
workhorse everyday card like the Chase Freedom Unlimited. So the Chase Freedom Unlimited earns 1.5x on everything. And
then for your rotating category card, it earns 5x on rotating categories. So it can be a nice way to rack up points. And
then you'd have your kind of premiumish travel card. So like the Chase Sapphire Preferred or Reserve, so that can help
you earn more on travel multipliers. And then more importantly, it would allow you to transfer out to partners. Uh you
can also kind of, you know, mix this up a bit. You can throw in business cards in there as well. So like the Chase Inc.
Cash, which earns 5x at office supply stores, the Chase Inc. Unlimited, which does the same thing as the Chase Freedom
Unlimited, but with a bigger signup bonus, and then also the Chase Inc. preferred, which is essentially the
Chase Sapphire Preferred, but a business version with a bit better of multipliers. Yes. So, the idea is that you're getting
multiple cards all from the Chase Ultimate rewards families, and each of them has different higher multipliers
for certain categories. Um, and although the Freedom Unlimited and Freedom Flex
as well as the Inc. cards are marketed as cash back, for example, they'll say, you know, the unlimited will be 1.5%
cash back on everything, 3% back on dining. they actually earn ultimate rewards points. So, you should be
thinking about it like 1.5x or 3x instead of 1.5%. So, the base value you
can get from cashing out your points will always be 1 cent per point. But, as Sean mentioned, if you have one of the
premium cards like the Sapphire Preferred, Sapphire Reserve, or Inca Preferred, um you can combine your points together uh and then uh transfer
to travel partners like airlines and hotels to get more value. Uh so Sean,

why don't you talk a little bit about the transfer partners for Chase and why people should pay attention to them? Yeah, so Chase has a decent amount of

yeah I really like that point you brought up you said the thing that went parel about the idea of like using a
credit card as if it were a debit card but not actually using the debit card like so many people I think are other
the mistaken understanding that if they don't need to carry a balance they should just use a debit card um not only
does that come with extra risks because uh you know if your money gets
stolen they don't have like the guarantees that the credit cards have you know sometimes you know if you go to
a hotel or you're filling up Pump they'll put like a big hold and that's going to be money taken out of your bank
account for who knows how long you know credit card insulates you from that and even just like the rewards you would be
missing out with a debit card I think this is a mindset more people need to understand no I totally agree I think a
lot of people they don't get into the credit card space because they they think like oh maybe I don't need the rewards but they also forget that they
are actively taking more Risk by not getting into the credit card space as you said like when you spend on a debit
card that's your money at risk if you need to charge back something like something wasn't as described that's
your money that's sitting there and waiting and if your money gets stolen from your debit card it it's gone until
they yes they'll give it back if it was you know a fraudulent charge but while you're waiting for that that's your
money if it's a credit card it's the bank's money it's not your money your your money safe and it's also this
access to credit is such an important thing because let's say if you don't have access to credit and something goes
wrong like you get stranded somewhere and you don't have enough cash in your bank account or unless you have One
debit card and then that account gets frozen what are you going to do you're stuck and this is even like what's so
nice about having credit cards from multiple Banks is that I know wherever I am in the world regardless of any

taught the university-sponsored course on credit card rewards I'm here today with my co-host Sherwin hey there I'm
Sherwin I attend Stanford University I got into this credit card and travel points in Miles game a bit over a year

differences for today to talk about credit cards today's topic does the
() 
chase 524 rule matter should you care about it I'll first begin by talking about what the chase 524 rule is Chase
has an application approval rule where if on your credit report you have opened
five or for more credit cards from any issuers in the last 24 months you will
automatically be denied for any Chase card now keep in mind that chase only pulls
your personal credit report so any business credit cards you opened with Chase or if other issuers that don't
report your credit report will now count toward your 524 status however any
business cards you try to open with Chase will be subject to the 524 rule in
order for you to get approved yes and we should be also careful here so there are
have been data points of people getting approved while you know having more credit cards open credit cards open in
the past 24 months so it can vary depending on the person but as a general rule you cannot get it past five cards
in 24 months and to be very clear about how we're defining this a lot of people Define this Pro incorrectly so when we
say five credit cards that you've opened in the past 24 months all that matters if it's is it if it's on your credit
report if it's not on your credit report it doesn't exist for the purposes of Chase so if I go and open the Capital
One Venture X today and I open six other cards today day and if they're not on my
credit report yet my 524 status is still unaffected so I can still get Chase cards now the inquiries I get usually
immediately show up on the report but the actual cards themselves can take a couple days to a couple weeks or an
amex's case up to two months to show up on my report so if it's not on my report it doesn't exist yes and it is five new
accounts not five inquiries so the inquiries usually are not relevant to
the you know the chase approval criteria as long as it's not overly excessive
()

We love to talk about the really sexy
credit card perks like lounge access,
airline status, hotel status, but what
about the infrastructure of a credit
card perk? What about the boring perks
that no one talks about, but that
actually could be the most useful of
them all? Today, we're going to talk
about purchase protections on credit
cards. This is a hugely underrated
benefit. My name is Sean and I'm here
with my co-host. Hi everyone, I'm
() 
Sherwin. Today on the Credit Card BS
podcast, all about purchase protections
and why you should be paying attention
to them. So, you know, this is the type
of perk, as Sean mentioned, that nobody
really talks about because they're kind
of the thing where you don't really care
about it um when you're deciding what
credit card to get until you come across
a day where you actually need to use it.
Then you find out it actually is on your
credit card and you're thankful that you
had it. So, there's kind of two main uh
purchase protection types we're going to
talk about. So, one is extended warranty
and then the second one is purchase
protection. So, let's talk about
extended warranty first. So the idea of
this is that let's say you buy an item
with a manufacturer's warranty of a
particular duration, but you use one of
these credit cards to buy it. The credit
card contains a benefit that will extend
the duration of that warranty for
another period of time. So let's say I
buy a new dishwasher and it has a 2-year
warranty and my credit card gives me
another 12 months or one year additional
warranty. And let's say my dishwasher
breaks uh two years and 6 months in and
my manufacturer will no longer give me
my money back because I've exceeded
that. But with my credit cards insurance
company, assuming I can find the
original statement of the purchase and
receipts and everything, uh they will
actually reimburse me the money as if
the manufacturer had covered it, which
is pretty awesome. So, this is typically
going to be a 12-month extension. So,
most of the AMX cards have 12 months
extension with a max claim of $10,000.
The same is mostly true for Bank of
America and Capital One as well. And
interestingly, Chase offers this on
mostly all the cards, even the ones with
no annual fee, like Freedom, Freedom
Unlimited, an income limited, or in
cash. Um, you might expect not to carry
this, but they all have um a 12-month
extended warranty with up to $10,000 per
claim. But you, this might surprise you,
but the best issuer for the extended
warranty is none other than City Bank
because their premier card and as well
as their double cash and custom cash
cards which have no annual fee have a
24month extended warranty. Michelle,
what are your thoughts? So, let's say I
had a city card with this 24-month
warranty.
Um, but let's let's say at the city of
Premiere, it's only earning me 1x points
and then I had like a venture X or MX
Bluebus Plus that are earning me 2x
points and I want to buy like some
important appliance for my home, right?
Would you take the, you know, 1x point
from the premiere but have that 24month
warranty or would you put on another
card that gives you more points but only
has the 12-month warranty? That's a good
question. I think it depends a little
bit on like the appliance I'm purchasing
and like the difficulty of like
repairing or replacing it potentially. I
will say you do have to factor in that
like when you're getting less points on
the city purchase, you're essentially
paying for the warranty extra, right?
Because the 1x extra be getting on the
Venture X, let's say it's like a $10,000
item. Okay, now I've just paid a minimum
of $100 for that extra year of warranty.
Now, that potentially could be worth it
because sometimes these credit card
warranties are like pretty generous in
that like they're not that much of a
headache to deal with. Sometimes it
depends, but maybe paying $100 is worth
it, maybe it isn't. It depends on the
appliance. Um, and then also like I know
no one's going to do this, but you
probably should like look a little bit
into the terms of the warranty because
there's going to be a whole list of
excluded items that are not included in
this claim. And so do not just go
blindly like buying things because like
oh it says it has a warranty. Maybe the
item you're buying doesn't have a

from your various credit accounts and
your credit score is just simply what
these agencies store about you and this
is very important because when I receive
a hard inquiry for example when I apply
for a credit card it's not like I
receive an inquiry on my general credit
score because there's no such thing as a
general credit score or even a general

pulls TransUnion so barlay is not going

totally separate agencies now some banks

you stored within that agency which is

someone who's like a points person and
loves loyalty programs and all that, but
simply put, Airbnb doesn't have a
loyalty program. And the problem with
this, right, the logic behind for Airbnb
on this is why are we giving customers
this rebate on things that don't really
matter, right? Why don't we just make

to come back? That's all great. One of
the things though that really does
benefit hotels with their loyalty

absolutely love them because what
happens is it's a form of payment almost
to the business traveler where my

this Marriott for 3 weeks a year
whatever right maybe I'm a consultant
maybe I'm traveling all the time I get
those points I get those elite
qualifying nights I get the status

points balance. So by me staying loyal
to this hotel brand, I end up amassing
this like big points balance because my
company's paying for it and then I get
to go on vacation with my family. And
yes, there's an argument to like why are

passing it on to the company because the

something that for this for the psych
psychology reasons of it, people really
like it. People like receiving a rebate.
It makes people pick a particular hotel
brand irrationally and it makes people
make irrational decisions. And I think
that's something always really to
remember in any type of business
application is that people are not
rational, right? People irrationally
choose things all the time. And I think

destroyed its arguably most valuable
perk. As Sean mentioned, the Venture X
is a very easily justifiable card, even
for the casual traveler. It comes in at

$100. So that brings effective annual
fee down to around neg5. Um, so you
know, they're almost paying you to keep
the card, which is why it was very hyped
up initially. Um, now another big reason

how generous it was with its lounge
access. So, not only does the primary

the Venture X also allowed you to add up

sounds good goodbyeIt's no question that over the past few
years, credit card annual fees have
pretty much skyrocketed. And it seems
like this trend is not slowing down at
all. So, I want to talk about why this
is happening, what I see the future of
credit card annual fees holding, and
kind of the overall way that you should
be thinking about this going forward. It
seems like even just a few years ago,
the MX Platinum was 550, the Sapphire
Reserve was $450. A lot of credit cards
around the $400 to $500 mark on the
premium segment. But recently, a lot of
these cards have been pressing into like
the eight to $900 segment, uh, with just
a few stragglers remaining around the
$400 to $500 mark. And we've seen this
huge rise of coupon book credit cards,
credit cards that have these, you know,
coupon book of credits where you're
getting, for example, the MX Platinum,
Uber credit, hotel credit, streaming
credits, a bunch of things that really
aren't that practical for a lot of
people. And we've seen other issuers
adopt this type of strategy. The recent
Sapphire Reserve refresh adopted this.
The City Strata Elite adopted this.
There's only a handful of cards that
really haven't taken this playbook. And
I predict that at some point they will.
And there's a few reasons for this, but
I think the main thing that's important
to talk about and the most obvious is
when MX is giving you an Uber credit or
one of these banks is giving you a
credit that goes to one or a small
selection of third party merchants, the
bank is not paying face value for that
credit. Not even close. I don't know the
exact economics of this, but they are
either getting it at a ridiculous
discount, nothing, or in fact being paid
for it depending on kind of the prestige
of the merchant and the value of the
credit. And so what's really interesting
about this is it does create this in

like a great example of this is Sachs,

you in an emergency? It's not candy.
It's your debit card. Debit cards are
one of the worst financial tools that
are available to you in the current
market. They essentially function very
similar to credit cards, but have none
of the benefits and all of the
downsides. When you go to a store and
you swipe a debit card, what ends up
happening is your money from your
personal bank account is debited
instantly.
In theory, right, seems fine. If I hand
someone cash, it's the same thing. But
when you swipe a credit card, it's
actually not your money at first that is
being paid to the merchant. It is the
bank's money, not yours. And there's a
fundamental difference here. And the
best way to think about it, one is when
you're walking around with a debit card,
in one sense, you're walking around with
however much checking balance you have
associated with that card. Yes, of
course there are like limitations, but
if you were walking around with a lot of
$5,000 in cash, you would probably feel
a little uneasy. And it kind of is in a
sense similar with a debit card because
if I go to a store with a debit card and
let's say someone found it and they
committed fraud on my card, my money is
locked up until the bank resolves the
dispute. Now, in theory, right, the
dispute will be resolved in your favor,
but the funds are already pulled from
your account. So, let's say I only had a
few hundred in my checking account,
maybe a few thousand. That money was
pulled, that money was taken aside, and
then what happens is someone goes and
like uses it. Well, great. Now, what
happens is I have no money. I have no
access to those funds. Even if I'm like,
"This is 100% fraud. This wasn't me."
Until that dispute is resolved, you
don't have access to the funds. A credit
card is entirely different because since
it is not your money being pulled, the
bank can't charge you anything for that
fraud fraudulent purchase until it is
the case that that dispute has been
resolved. And of course, if it's
resolved in your favor, nothing's going
to be charged. If it resolved against
you, then yes, you may be charged
something, but that's like, you know,
only if it really wasn't fraud. And so
what happens is it's an extremely
dangerous tool to be carrying around
with you because you just have no perks.
Your money is gone. It's essentially
tied to your account. That's why credit
cards are such a much better tool
because they have this layer of
protection. And beyond just this general
like security aspect of it and the fact
that debit cards are just not secure at
all compared to credit cards for the
consumer, um there's just no additional
benefits, right? debit cards because I
believe it's the DoddFrank Act, they are
capped on their interchange fees. So,
banks are not allowed to provide
rewards. I mean, aren't really
economically able to provide rewards to
people using them. The exceptions are
some small NEO banks because of the way
the law is written. If your bank holds a
certain amount of assets um or below a
certain amount of assets, you are
allowed to charge a small interchange
fee, which is why there are some rewards
debit cards out there. Uh but they are
much less common than credit cards. And
so, this means really you have no perks.
um you know, credit cards, especially on
the premium side, you'll have like
purchase protections, extended warranty,
return protections, points earning,
which is obviously is a huge difference
in the rebates, and that you're
literally just like wasting like 1 to 3%
of your money if you're not using a
credit card. Um, and so it's it's this
big differential of you're just throwing
money away and you have no protections.
And what will happen, of course, there's
inevitably going to be someone that says
credit card fees are immoral. This
system of protections and perks only
exists because you have people that are
paying cash into the system that are
using debit cards in the system with no
interchange fees at merchants that are
now like essentially funding the credit
card rewards because there's an
interchange fee. This interchange fee
the merchant is paying meaning the price
of goods is being raised and it's being
passed along to the consumer. And there
is an argument to that. But I think my
counterargument is the very DoddFrank
Act that reduced the debit card
interchange fees. There's no evidence
that because of that law that the price
of any goods were reduced. And this kind
of tracks logically because many
merchants, right, if they're selling an
item for $2.99 and they want to have
this nice pricing bucket, a 3%
transaction fee makes it very weird to
change that bucket. um they're not going
to raise it to like $35 or something
because of the interchange fee. And I
would also argue there's a certain, you
know, because of the protections
associated with electronic payments.
Yes, this is also true on debit cards,
but because of the fact that like with
credit cards, you can't have someone
like shortchanging you at the register,
no one stealing your cash, you can't be
robbed as a as an inerson store. Uh
there's a and you don't have to handle
the cash. There's a certain operational
cost savings that merchants are also
benefiting from. And so the argument
really I would make is that there's not
a real piece of evidence that removing
credit card interchange fees would in
fact reduce the price of goods in really
any major capacity because what would
really just happen is merchants would
likely just be pocketing the difference.
Now, right, would you rather have the
credit card company pocket the
difference or the merchant? Obviously,
like that's a different argument, right?
Small stores totally get that. Like you
want to have them, you know, be taking
more of their actual revenue. But my
main argument here is that credit cards
are a much more powerful tool for so
many people just because the fact that
you have this extra layer of protection.
And of course there is like one very
very notable exception. Um you should

that's just stupid because you'll be

nextI've got a take that a lot of people are
going to disagree with. I think a lot
more people should complain a lot more
at hotels, especially luxury hotels.
Okay, hear me out. I'm not saying that
people should go around and be mean to
staff and complain about stupid small
things that don't matter just to get
compensation. I am saying that the only
way to give a hotel feedback sometimes
is to complain in a nice way and let
them know what went wrong and what could
be done to fix it. Let's say you have a
hotel stay and a few things go wrong.
You don't really care about it that
much, right? Maybe like you asked for
some ice, they didn't bring you ice,
service was slow at the restaurant,
little housekeeping mishap, etc. And you
know, they're all like small things. You
tell yourself, you know what, no big
deal. Doesn't really matter. But then
you go tell your friend, hey, you know,
yeah, my hotel stay here. Like they're
asking about the hotel and they're like,
hey, should I stay here? And you're
like, yeah, it was pretty good, but you
know, these things XYZ went wrong. And
then your friend goes, yeah, I don't
know. I'm not going to stay there. That
seems weird, right? Like the hotel, yes,
like they messed up on those things, but
now your friend doesn't go there. The
hotel's lost business. Whereas maybe if
you would have told the hotel about what
had gone wrong, maybe they would have
gone, this is totally not normal for us.
This never happens. I'm very sorry.
Let's give you 10,000 points as, you
know, thank you for bringing this up to
us and let's fix it for you. And you'd
be leaving that hotel and go, actually
that wow, that they handled that really
well. And then you tell your friend and
your friend goes, oh wow, I'm impressed.
That's like a really good way to handle
it. That's good service. And your rest
of your stay was great. And it's you got
some compensation. Maybe your stay was
better. the hotel got to know, oh god
damn it, like we can't mess this up
anymore. And then now you tell your
friend and they get extra business. And
the point I'm trying to illustrate is I
think a lot of people find it to be
polite to just not say anything and let
things in their head kind of like bubble
up and then they like tell it to other
people. They leave it in a review or
they just talk about their experience
with someone else. And you know, it's
like I understand that like yes, it's
easier to do that and you know, if a
hotel messes up on something, they still
messed up on it. So, it's like fair
game. But I think the better way to
handle a lot of these situations is to
just talk to someone at the hotel. Even
if you don't want to tell them in
person, leave it in the survey feedback.
Leave it in some anonymous form because
you then give them a chance to fix it.
You give them a chance to have a better
service standard and I think it's a
better situation for everyone. What ends
up happening is people get so afraid to
talk to a staff member. They get so
afraid to leave negative feedback
because you feel like you're being rude.
And that's not really true. What's rude
is to let a small thing bubble up in
your head and then you tell someone
about it and then they lose business
because of it. You should have given
them a chance. Maybe they would have
fixed it. There's a lot of hotels that
when there's a service mishap and
something goes wrong, handle it
remarkably well. I'll give a few
